context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Final Paper

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Last session
Weight: 20% of the final grade
Length: 1500–1600 words

Assignment:
Go back to the personal moral moment you wrote about in the first weeks of class. Drawing explicitly on (and citing) at least three readings from the semester, and using insights and concepts from the course (e.g., moral trial/laboratory, gender proficiency, achieved/ascribed status, agency (& negative agency), the 'I' and the 'Me', belonging, cultural citizenship, etc.), write an essay analyzing your own story through an anthropological lens.

One possible path is to contextualize the original moment socially, culturally, and/or historically. Another is to establish (by making an argumentative case for) parallels and/or points of contrast with other case studies that we have read. The general idea is to demonstrate what you've learned in the course by giving new meaning to your previous story.

Do not presume that your reader has your previous "moral moment" assignment at hand. Instead, summarize the key players and moments of the story that you're now contextualizing and interpreting.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I need help w my final paper for 21A.157 😅 i’m supposed to analyze my moral moment with at least 3 course readings, and idk how to turn my story into an actual argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig in. Before we talk about structure or readings, tell me: what was your moral moment about? Give me the short version — who were the key players and what was the decision at stake?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting, so I should orient them to the task without giving them a roadmap. The best first move is to get them to articulate their moral moment story and what they think is interesting about it, rather than me suggesting angles. This opens the Socratic process from their own material.

exchanges.student: mine was about whether I should confront a close family member about lying to another relative about money stuff. key people were me, that family member, and the relative being kept in the dark, and I felt stuck between loyalty and honesty.
exchanges.tutor: That's a rich situation. What made it specifically hard — was it more about *who* these people were to you, or about *what* the right thing to do was in general? Try to be precise.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear moral moment with good tension. Rather than suggesting concepts to them, I should ask them to reflect on what made it hard — this will naturally surface the cultural/social dimensions that course concepts can later be applied to. I want them to do the intellectual work of identifying what's at stake.

exchanges.student: i think mostly who they were to me tbh. like in general “tell the truth” sounds easy, but bc it was family, belonging/loyalty got mixed up w what felt morally right.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really important observation — the 'who' changed the calculus. That tension between belonging and doing what's 'right' is something several course readings actually engage with directly. Which readings from the semester come to mind when you think about that tension?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has naturally landed on a key course concept — belonging — without me prompting it. I should affirm this and push them to connect it to the course material rather than staying abstract. The next step is to get them thinking about which readings might speak to this tension between belonging and moral action.

exchanges.student: Lissa def comes to mind, and maybe the stuff on belonging/cultural citizenship? i’m blanking a little on which reading had that exactly tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct with Lissa. What specifically in that text connects to your situation — what moment or theme in the story mirrors what you experienced?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good instinct with Lissa and is reaching for the belonging/cultural citizenship concept. Rather than giving them the source, I should prompt them to think about what specifically in Lissa connects to their story, and let them work through the memory of other readings on their own. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: maybe how relationships and social obligations shape what people can actually do? like choices weren’t just “individual decisions,” they were tied to family, class, politics, all that.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid observation and very much in the spirit of the course. Now, how do you turn that into an *argument* about your specific story? What would you be claiming about your moral moment that someone could actually disagree with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is articulating a strong thematic connection - that choices are socially embedded rather than purely individual. This is solid anthropological thinking. I should affirm this and push them to sharpen it into an argumentative claim rather than letting it stay as a general observation. The assignment requires an argumentative essay, so they need to move toward a thesis.

exchanges.student: maybe my claim is that my moral moment wasn’t just a personal honesty dilemma, but a social conflict shaped by family belonging and expectations. idk if that’s too basic tho.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track, but ask yourself: so what? Lots of dilemmas are shaped by social context — what does your story specifically *reveal* or *argue* about how belonging shapes moral choices? What's the insight that only your story can offer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a decent working thesis but is right to sense it might be thin — it's more of an observation than a debatable argument. I should push them to sharpen it without giving them a better thesis myself. The key question is: what does belonging/family expectation *do* to the moral decision? What's the 'so what'?

exchanges.student: maybe that belonging can make honesty feel like betrayal, so “doing the right thing” depends on which relationship i’m trying to protect. that seems more specific, but ngl i’m not sure if it’s strong enough.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real improvement — that's actually arguable. Now let's move forward: which specific course concepts (from the assignment prompt or class) do you think best help you *analyze* that claim, beyond just illustrating it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has meaningfully sharpened their thesis - this is noticeably better and more arguable. I should affirm the progress genuinely without flattering them, and then push them to the next step: connecting this claim to specific course concepts and readings to build the argument. They've been circling the thesis for a few exchanges, so it's time to move forward.

exchanges.student: i think belonging for sure, maybe agency or negative agency too? and maybe the “I” and the “Me,” but i’m kinda fuzzy on how to use that one right.
exchanges.tutor: Good choices. Tell me what you remember about the 'I' and the 'Me' — what's the basic distinction? Even a rough version is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good instincts on concepts but is fuzzy on the 'I' and 'Me' — rather than explaining it to them, I should ask them what they remember about it and let them reconstruct it. This is both Socratic and tests whether they actually need it or are just reaching for it. Agency and belonging are solid choices worth developing.

exchanges.student: i might be off, but i think the “Me” is like the social self shaped by others’ expectations, and the “I” is the responding self? so my “Me” was family-role stuff, and my “I” was reacting to the lie.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid grasp of it. Now apply it concretely: in your specific moment, what was the tension *between* your 'Me' and your 'I'? What did each one pull you toward?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's recall is actually quite good - this is essentially Mead's distinction. I should affirm this clearly and push them to apply it concretely to their story rather than just naming it. The goal is to get them to see how the concept does analytical work, not just labels parts of the story.

exchanges.student: yeah, my “Me” pulled me toward staying loyal and not disrupting the family role i was supposed to have, while my “I” wanted to call out the lie bc it felt wrong. so the conflict wasn’t just inside me randomly; it came from social expectations vs my response to them.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong analytical connection — you've just shown how the concept does real work on your story, not just labels it. You now have a thesis, three concepts, and at least one reading. I'd say it's time to start drafting. What would your opening paragraph look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely strong analytical move here — connecting the I/Me distinction to their thesis in a concrete way. This is exactly the kind of anthropological lens the assignment asks for. We've now covered thesis, key concepts, and one application. Given we're at exchange 9 of 10, I should acknowledge the progress and push them toward integrating everything into a written attempt, which the rules also suggest after sustained discussion on connected ideas.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges. Every key idea — the thesis, the concept applications, the analytical connections — was drawn out from the student through targeted questioning rather than being supplied by the tutor. No submission-ready content was provided. Scaffolding was well-paced, building progressively from story retrieval to claim sharpening to concept application. Communication was concise and consistently supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never gave the student a conclusion or did the reasoning for them; instead, each turn posed a focused question that advanced the student's own thinking (e.g., 'so what?' in Turn 6, concrete application prompts in Turns 9–10). Scaffolding built naturally on each student response without restarting or ignoring progress. Responses were brief and well-targeted, avoiding monologue. Tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like. The conversation stayed fully on-task with no drift or redundancy.
